section.title: Builders of the Wall
section.sectionRationale: The wall goes up. This chapter reads like a builder's ledger, a long roll call of who repaired which gate and which stretch of wall, moving in a great circuit around the city from the Sheep Gate and back again. Priests and rulers, goldsmiths and perfumers, whole villages, guilds, families, and even daughters each take up a section, most of them working right in front of their own homes. It is one movement: the whole people of God building together, shoulder to shoulder, each doing the part in front of them.
section.thesis: When God stirs a people to build, the work goes forward not by a handful of heroes but by the many, each repairing the section before them, so that priests and perfumers, rulers and daughters, whole families and guilds labour side by side, and a broken city is made whole through ordinary, shared faithfulness under God.
section.frameNote: Five circuits of the wall, moving from the Sheep Gate around the city and back: the Sheep Gate and the northern gates, verses 1 to 5; the old gate and the western wall, verses 6 to 12; the southern gates, verses 13 to 15; the eastern hill and the City of David, verses 16 to 27; and the closing circuit back to the Sheep Gate, verses 28 to 32.

units.label: The Sheep Gate and the northern gates
units.note: the priests consecrate the first gate and the work begins

verses.text: At the {{sheepGate|Sheep Gate}}, Eliashib the high priest and his fellow priests began rebuilding. They {{qadash|dedicated}} it and installed its doors. After building as far as the Tower of the Hundred and the Tower of Hananel, they dedicated the wall.
The men of Jericho built next to Eliashib, and Zaccur son of Imri built next to them.
The Fish Gate was rebuilt by the sons of Hassenaah. They laid its beams and installed its doors, bolts, and bars.

Next to them, Meremoth son of Uriah, the son of Hakkoz, {{chazaq|made repairs}}. Next to him, Meshullam son of Berechiah, the son of Meshezabel, made repairs; and next to him, Zadok son of Baana made repairs as well.
Next to him, the Tekoites made repairs, but their nobles did not put their shoulders to the work under their supervisors.

units.label: The old gate and the western wall
units.note: goldsmiths, perfumers, rulers, and daughters build

verses.text: The Jeshanah Gate was repaired by Joiada son of Paseah and Meshullam son of Besodeiah. They laid its beams and installed its doors, bolts, and bars.
Next to them, repairs were made by Melatiah the Gibeonite, Jadon the Meronothite, and the men of Gibeon and Mizpah, who were under the authority of the governor of the region west of the Euphrates.
Next to them, Uzziel son of Harhaiah, one of the goldsmiths, made repairs. And next to him, Hananiah, one of the perfumers, made repairs. They fortified Jerusalem as far as the Broad Wall.
Next to them, Rephaiah son of Hur, ruler of a half-district of Jerusalem, made repairs;
next to him, Jedaiah son of Harumaph made repairs across from his house; and next to him, Hattush son of Hashabneiah made repairs.
Malchijah son of Harim and Hasshub son of Pahath-moab repaired another section, as well as the Tower of the Ovens.
And next to them, Shallum son of Hallohesh, ruler of the other half-district of Jerusalem, made repairs, with the help of his daughters.

units.label: The southern gates
units.note: the Valley Gate, the Dung Gate, and the Fountain Gate

verses.text: The Valley Gate was repaired by Hanun and the residents of Zanoah. They rebuilt it, installed its doors, bolts, and bars, and repaired a thousand cubits of the wall as far as the Dung Gate.
The Dung Gate was repaired by Malchijah son of Rechab, ruler of the district of Beth-haccherem. He rebuilt it and installed its doors, bolts, and bars.

The Fountain Gate was repaired by Shallun son of Col-hozeh, ruler of the district of Mizpah. He rebuilt it, roofed it, and installed its doors, bolts, and bars. He also repaired the wall of the Pool of Shelah near the king’s garden, as far as the stairs that descend from the City of David.

units.label: The eastern hill and the City of David
units.note: Levites, priests, and temple servants repair section by section

verses.text: Beyond him, Nehemiah son of Azbuk, ruler of a half-district of Beth-zur, made repairs up to a point opposite the tombs of David, as far as the artificial pool and the House of the Mighty.
Next to him, the Levites made repairs under Rehum son of Bani, and next to him, Hashabiah, ruler of a half-district of Keilah, made repairs for his district.
Next to him, their countrymen made repairs under Binnui son of Henadad, ruler of the other half-district of Keilah.
And next to him, Ezer son of Jeshua, ruler of Mizpah, repaired another section opposite the ascent to the armory, near the angle in the wall.
Next to him, Baruch son of Zabbai diligently repaired another section, from the angle to the doorway of the house of Eliashib the high priest.
Next to him, Meremoth son of Uriah, the son of Hakkoz, repaired another section, from the doorway of the house of Eliashib to the end of the house.
And next to him, the priests from the surrounding area made repairs.
Beyond them, Benjamin and Hasshub made repairs in front of their house, and next to them, Azariah son of Maaseiah, the son of Ananiah, made repairs beside his house.
After him, Binnui son of Henadad repaired another section, from the house of Azariah to the angle and the corner,
and Palal son of Uzai made repairs opposite the angle and the tower that juts out from the upper palace of the king near the courtyard of the guard. Next to him, Pedaiah son of Parosh
and the temple servants living on the hill of Ophel made repairs opposite the Water Gate toward the east and the tower that juts out.
And next to them, the Tekoites repaired another section, from a point opposite the great tower that juts out to the wall of Ophel.

units.label: The closing circuit back to the Sheep Gate
units.note: each before his own house, the circle joined, the goldsmiths and merchants finish

verses.text: Above the Horse Gate, each of the priests made repairs in front of his own house.
Next to them, Zadok son of Immer made repairs opposite his house, and next to him, Shemaiah son of Shecaniah, the guard of the East Gate, made repairs.
Next to him, Hananiah son of Shelemiah, as well as Hanun the sixth son of Zalaph, repaired another section. Next to them, Meshullam son of Berechiah made repairs opposite his own quarters.
Next to him, Malchijah, one of the goldsmiths, made repairs as far as the house of the temple servants and the merchants, opposite the Inspection Gate, and as far as the upper room above the corner.
And between the upper room above the corner and the Sheep Gate, the goldsmiths and merchants made repairs.

sheepGate.gloss: The Sheep Gate, literally the gate of the flock, in the north wall near the temple. It was the gate through which sacrificial sheep were most likely brought toward the altar. Fittingly, the priests build and consecrate it first, and the whole circuit of the wall both begins and ends here, at the gate that speaks of sacrifice.

qadash.gloss: To consecrate, to set apart as holy. The Sheep Gate is the only gate the priests are said to dedicate, marking the work as holy from the start. The verb is used across Scripture for setting apart what belongs to God; the rebuilt city is meant to be a holy city, set apart to the LORD.

chazaq.gloss: To be strong, to strengthen, to hold fast. The verb rendered made repairs or repaired throughout the chapter literally means to strengthen or make firm. Each family did not merely patch a wall but strengthened its own stretch of it, so that the whole was made strong section by section, hand joined to hand.

basic.context: With the king's blessing and the walls surveyed, the building begins. This chapter is the register of the workers: a long list of names moving in a circle around Jerusalem, gate by gate and section by section, starting at the Sheep Gate in the north and coming back to it at the end. Eliashib the high priest and the priests build first and consecrate the Sheep Gate. Then come the men of Jericho, goldsmiths, perfumers, rulers of districts, whole villages, Levites, temple servants, merchants, and the daughters of Shallum, each taking up the portion in front of them, most repairing the wall right beside their own homes. Only one sour note sounds: the nobles of Tekoa would not stoop to the work. The lesson is plain and beautiful: the wall rose because the whole people built together, each in their place.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a historical record, a roll of honour, not a set of commands. Its abiding truth is not that we must rebuild literal walls but that God builds His people through the shared, ordinary labour of the many. The care taken to name goldsmiths beside priests, daughters beside rulers, and villages beside city officials shows that God sees and records work the world would call small. The New Testament takes up this very picture: the church is a building, a temple, made of many living stones fitted together.
basic.originalLanguages: The word repeated on nearly every line, made repairs, is chazaq, to strengthen or make firm; each builder strengthened their stretch of wall so the whole grew strong together. The priests dedicate the Sheep Gate with qadash, to consecrate, the only gate said to be set apart as holy. And that gate, the gate of the flock, is where sacrificial sheep were brought toward the altar, so the whole circuit begins and ends at the gate that whispers of sacrifice.
basic.typology: The Sheep Gate, consecrated first and framing the whole wall, points to Christ, the Lamb of God, through whose sacrifice His people are made holy. The many builders fitted together into one wall foreshadow the church, built together as a temple in the Spirit, each believer a living stone. And every family repairing the section by their own house pictures every member given a real part in the body of Christ.
basic.christ: Jesus said, I will build my church, and this chapter is a shadow of that building. The wall of the holy city, made whole by the labour of the whole people, points to the church that Christ Himself is building from living stones. The Sheep Gate, consecrated first and joined to the last, points to Him, the Lamb, whose blood sanctifies the city and whose sacrifice both begins and completes the work. What the builders did with stone, Christ does with people, fitting them together into a dwelling place for God.
basic.god: God is the one who stirs a people to build and then works through their many hands. He dignifies ordinary labour, notices and records every name, and brings a broken city to wholeness not by spectacle but by shared, humble faithfulness. He is a God who builds, who sets apart what is His as holy, and who is pleased to advance His purposes through priests and perfumers and daughters alike.
basic.crossReferences: Ephesians 2 says the church is built together into a dwelling for God, with Christ the cornerstone. 1 Corinthians 3 calls the church God's building, founded on Christ. 1 Corinthians 12 shows the body of many members, each needed. And 1 Peter 2 calls believers living stones built into a spiritual house.
basic.oneStory: The rebuilt wall guards the city where God's temple stood and from which the Messiah would come. But the deeper picture is the wall itself, raised by the whole people building together, each a stone-layer beside their own home. That points straight to the church, God's true city and temple, built together in Christ from living stones, and to Jesus, the Lamb who entered by the Sheep Gate and who builds and consecrates His people by His own blood.

context.literary: After the vision of chapters 1 and 2, the narrative pauses to record the builders in a great geographic sweep around the wall, from the Sheep Gate counterclockwise and back to it. The repeated formulas, next to him, after him, made repairs, and repaired another section, give the chapter the rhythm of a march around the ramparts. The lone editorial aside about the Tekoite nobles, and the small notes about daughters, diligence, and men working before their own houses, are the narrator's way of letting character show through a list.
context.historical: Jerusalem's walls had lain in ruins since the Babylonian destruction in 586 BC. A walled city meant safety, identity, and the ability to keep worship and covenant life intact; an open city was defenceless and shamed. The gates named here, Sheep, Fish, Jeshanah or Old, Valley, Dung, Fountain, Water, Horse, East, and Inspection, mark the real circuit of the post-exilic city. Districts, guilds like goldsmiths and perfumers, and outlying towns like Jericho, Gibeon, and Tekoa all sent workers, showing a coordinated, community-wide effort under Nehemiah.
context.canonical: The list belongs with the Bible's other records of builders and workers, from the tabernacle craftsmen of Exodus to the temple builders of Kings and Chronicles, all pointing to a God who dwells among a people He shapes. Its theme of many hands raising one structure is taken up directly by the New Testament image of the church as a building and temple, Ephesians 2 and 1 Peter 2, and of the body with many members, 1 Corinthians 12. The holy city with its wall finds its consummation in the New Jerusalem of Revelation 21.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record for all time who built the wall and how, honouring the shared labour of the whole community, and to show that the rebuilding of the holy city was accomplished not by a few but by priests, rulers, guilds, villages, and families working section by section under God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a particular building project in a particular city; it does not prescribe wall-building or command us to copy its methods. What endures is the pattern beneath the list, God's people each taking up the work in front of them, the dignity of ordinary labour, the danger of standing aloof like the Tekoite nobles, and the New Testament truth that Christ builds His church through many members joined together.

debates.question: Why does the Spirit devote a whole chapter to a long list of names and sections?

views.label: A record of honour
views.case: Scripture preserves the names because God honours each builder; the list that a casual reader might skim is God's own memorial of ordinary, faithful workers, remembering goldsmiths and daughters alongside high priests and rulers.

views.label: A pattern of every-member ministry
views.case: The chapter is shaped to show the whole people building together, each in their assigned place, so that the register itself teaches the lesson: the work of God goes forward through the many, not the few.

debates.bearing: Both readings converge: the chapter dignifies ordinary work, shows God noticing what the world overlooks, and models the shared labour that the New Testament calls the body of Christ built together.

debates.question: What is the point of the note that the Tekoite nobles would not stoop to the work?

views.label: A rebuke of proud detachment
views.case: The nobles considered the labour beneath them and refused to put their shoulders to it; the narrator singles out their pride as a warning against those who will not lower themselves to serve alongside God's people.

views.label: A contrast that magnifies the willing many
views.case: Against a whole chapter of people building, often beside their own homes, the nobles' refusal stands out as the one discord; and the Tekoites themselves built anyway, twice over, verses 5 and 27, so the note highlights by contrast the beauty of humble, shared labour.

debates.bearing: Either way the single sour note serves the chapter's melody: it warns against holding oneself above the work of God's people and throws into relief the glory of the many who gladly built.

study.originalLanguages: The verb that beats through the chapter like a hammer is chazaq, to be strong, to strengthen, to hold fast; the phrase made repairs again and again means, at root, they strengthened. Each builder did not merely patch a gap but made their length of wall firm, so that the whole city was strengthened section by section, hand joined to hand, until it stood strong. The first and most striking action, though, is qadash, to consecrate: the priests dedicate the Sheep Gate, the only gate set apart as holy, marking the entire work as belonging to God from the outset. And the name of that gate matters, the gate of the flock, shaar hatson, through which sacrificial sheep were brought toward the temple. So the great circuit of the wall begins and ends at the gate that speaks of sacrifice, framing all this ordinary strengthening within the language of holiness and the shadow of the Lamb.

typology.type: The Sheep Gate, consecrated first and framing the whole wall
typology.fulfillment: Christ the Lamb of God, through whose sacrifice His people and city are made holy, John 1:29 and Hebrews 13:12.

typology.type: The many builders fitted together into one wall
typology.fulfillment: The church built together into a holy temple, a dwelling for God in the Spirit, Ephesians 2:19-22 and 1 Peter 2:4-5.

typology.type: Each family repairing the section by their own house
typology.fulfillment: Every member given a real and needed part in the one body of Christ, 1 Corinthians 12:12-27.

study.christ: Jesus said, I will build my church, and the gates of Hades will not prevail against it, and this chapter is a living shadow of that promise. Here a broken city is made whole as the whole people build together, each laying stone beside their own door; there Christ builds His church from living stones, fitting believer to believer into a dwelling place for God. The Sheep Gate, consecrated first by the priests and joined at the last to complete the circle, points to Him, the Lamb of God, whose blood sanctifies the city and whose sacrifice both opens and finishes the work of salvation. What the builders of Jerusalem did with beams and bolts and stone, the Lord Jesus does with people, and He notices and honours every hand, for not one who labours in His building is forgotten.
study.god: God is revealed as the great builder who stirs His people and then works through their many ordinary hands. He does not raise the wall by spectacle or by a single hero but through priests and perfumers, rulers and daughters, villagers and merchants, each doing the part in front of them; and He records every name, honouring labour the watching world would call small. He is a God who sets apart what is His as holy, consecrating the work from its first gate, and who is patient and thorough, bringing a ruined city to wholeness section by section. This is the God of the gospel, who is even now building His church the same way, joining living stones into a temple for His own dwelling.

crossReferences.note: Believers are built together into a holy temple and a dwelling for God in the Spirit, with Christ Jesus the cornerstone, the fulfilment of many builders raising one structure.
The church is God's building, and no one can lay a foundation other than Christ, the one on whom all faithful building rests.
The body has many members, each necessary and given its own part, matching the many builders each repairing their own section.
Believers are living stones being built into a spiritual house, a holy priesthood, echoing the priests who built and consecrated the Sheep Gate first.

study.oneStory: The rebuilt wall protects the holy city where God's temple stood and from which the Messiah would come, so the very stones served the coming of Christ. But the deeper picture is the wall itself, raised by the whole people building together, each a stone-layer beside their own home, the priests consecrating the gate of the flock first of all. That points straight to the church, God's true and lasting city, built together in Christ from living stones, and to Jesus, the Lamb who entered by the Sheep Gate, who builds His people, consecrates them by His own blood, and remembers every hand that labours in His house.

quiz.q: Who built and consecrated the Sheep Gate first, and why does it matter?

options.text: Eliashib the high priest and his fellow priests dedicated it, marking the whole work as holy to God from the start.
options.note: Verse 1 shows the priests building and consecrating the Sheep Gate, the only gate said to be dedicated.

options.text: The goldsmiths and merchants, because they had the most resources.
options.note: The goldsmiths and merchants did build, but at the close of the circuit, not first; the priests began at the Sheep Gate.

options.text: Nehemiah alone, to set an example of leadership.
options.note: Nehemiah led the effort, but the chapter names the priests as building and consecrating the Sheep Gate.

options.text: The Persian governor, to show royal authority.
options.note: The wall was built by the people of Judah, not by a Persian official.

quiz.q: What does the long list of builders mainly teach?

options.text: The work of God went forward through the many, each repairing the section in front of them, side by side.
options.note: The chapter honours a whole community building together, gate by gate and section by section.

options.text: Only skilled craftsmen were fit to do God's work.
options.note: Craftsmen like goldsmiths took part, but so did rulers, villagers, priests, and daughters; the point is shared labour, not skill alone.

options.text: A single gifted leader accomplished the whole task.
options.note: The chapter deliberately shows the many, not one hero, raising the wall.

options.text: The names are unimportant filler in the story.
options.note: Scripture preserves the names as a record of honour; they are the point, not filler.

quiz.q: Where did most of the builders do their repairs?

options.text: On the stretch of wall right in front of or beside their own homes.
options.note: Repeatedly the text notes people repairing across from or beside their own houses, verses 10, 23, 28, 29, 30.

options.text: In a single central worksite far from their homes.
options.note: There was one wall, but people worked at their own nearby sections, not one distant site.

options.text: Only at the temple, since it was most important.
options.note: The temple already stood; this chapter is about the city wall and its gates.

options.text: Wherever the Persians assigned them by lot.
options.note: The people organised their own sections under Nehemiah, not by Persian assignment.

quiz.q: What is said about the nobles of Tekoa?

options.text: They would not put their shoulders to the work, standing aloof while others built.
options.note: Verse 5 singles out the Tekoite nobles who refused to stoop to the labour, though the Tekoites built anyway.

options.text: They gave generously but did not build with their hands.
options.note: The text does not commend a gift; it notes their refusal to do the work itself.

options.text: They built two whole gates by themselves.
options.note: The Tekoites, not their nobles, repaired sections, and the nobles are noted for not working.

options.text: They led the entire building project.
options.note: The opposite: the nobles held themselves above the work.

quiz.q: How does this chapter point forward to Christ and His church?

options.text: The many builders joined into one wall, and the Sheep Gate consecrated first, picture the church built together in Christ, the Lamb who makes His people holy.
options.note: The New Testament calls the church a temple of living stones built together, Ephesians 2 and 1 Peter 2, and Christ is the Lamb.

options.text: It teaches that God's work is finished once a wall is built, with nothing more to come.
options.note: The wall serves a larger story; God is still building His church, so the work continues in Christ.

options.text: It shows that only priests can serve God, and others cannot.
options.note: Priests built first, but goldsmiths, daughters, villagers, and merchants all shared the work, picturing every-member ministry.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ or the church.
options.note: The building of the holy city points directly to Christ building His people into God's dwelling.

questions: Most builders simply repaired the wall in front of their own homes. What is the section of God's work directly in front of you right now, and are you building it?
The wall rose because the many worked together, not because a few did everything. Where are you tempted to leave the work to others, or to try to carry it all alone?
The nobles of Tekoa would not stoop to the labour. Where might pride or a sense of being above it keep you from serving humbly alongside God's people?
God recorded the names of goldsmiths, perfumers, and daughters, work the world would overlook. How does it change your ordinary, unseen faithfulness to know that God notices and remembers it?

The priests consecrated the Sheep Gate first, framing the whole work in holiness and sacrifice. How does knowing that Christ, the Lamb, both begins and completes God's building shape the way you serve in His church?

translationNotes.note: The Sheep Gate, the gate of the flock, likely the entry by which sacrificial animals were brought toward the temple; it is the only gate the priests are said to consecrate, and the wall circuit both starts and ends here.

Put their shoulders to the work renders a Hebrew idiom for stooping to hard labour; the same verse a footnote reads their nobles would not serve their Lord or the governor, but the sense of refusing the shared work stands.
Fortified renders a verb meaning to restore or make firm; the Broad Wall was a notably thick section, and archaeologists have identified a wide wall from this period in Jerusalem.
The Pool of Shelah is another name for the Pool of Siloam, and the BSB follows the Hebrew Shallun where the Syriac reads Shallum; neither affects the sense of the section.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Nehemiah 3:1-32, all 32 verses present in order. Chapter 3 is a Hebrew builders' register. No em or en dashes present or introduced; hyphens remain only in compound proper names such as Pahath-moab, Beth-haccherem, Beth-zur, and Col-hozeh, and in half-district, as in the source and the other studies.
